title: Status of experiments and simulations on scaling problems in turbulence
speaker: Katepalli Sreenivasan
abstract: We present an elementary summary of known results, and open questions, on scaling problems in hydrodynamic turbulence in three dimensions. The goal is to provide some background for the two talks to follow, and summarize Victor Yakhot's theory, depending on the time available.

proof_stages.label: Data regime and numerical apparatus
proof_stages.summary: The speaker establishes the experimental, atmospheric, and periodic-box DNS databases, their Reynolds-number range, resolution, forcing choices, and limitations as the empirical basis for judging finite-Reynolds-number objections.

proof_stages.label: Kolmogorov similarity benchmark and anomalous exponents
proof_stages.summary: Kolmogorov similarity would make velocity structure functions scale as r^{p/3} in the inertial range, but measured anomalous exponents zeta_p make different moments independent scaling problems subject to isotropy and dissipation caveats.

proof_stages.label: Failure of universal spectral collapse at the Kolmogorov scale
proof_stages.summary: Dissipation-range spectra fail to collapse under the traditional k eta normalization but collapse much better with k eta R_lambda^{-1/2}, suggesting a Reynolds-dependent small-scale cutoff and exponential spectral tails.

proof_stages.label: Local dissipation-scale field and eta-infinity cutoff
proof_stages.summary: By treating the Kolmogorov scale as a fluctuating local field, using local Reynolds-number balance and structure-function relations, the talk derives a smallest scale eta_infinity comparable to eta R_lambda^{-1/2} and checks whether mean-free-path thermal noise threatens this scale.

proof_stages.label: Saturation and component-dependence of velocity scaling
proof_stages.summary: Transverse structure functions, PDF collapses, and very high order longitudinal data indicate saturation of anomalous exponents at component-dependent levels, raising the question of what turbulent scaling means beyond the Kolmogorov picture.

proof_stages.label: Eulerian circulation loops and high-moment area rule
proof_stages.summary: For circulation around Eulerian loops, rectangular-loop data largely support Migdal's area-rule tail form while perimeter-dependent corrections show that exact area universality fails away from the high-moment PDF tails.

proof_stages.label: Loop-geometry tests and circulation scaling exponents
proof_stages.summary: Figure-eight and nonplanar minimal-surface loop tests probe which area controls circulation, and square-loop moments exhibit anomalous but weaker-than-velocity-structure-function departures from Kolmogorov scaling with increasing-Reynolds-number trends toward linear behavior.

proof_stages.label: Synthesis and open scaling questions
proof_stages.summary: The conclusion is that Kolmogorov universality fails in spectra and velocity structure functions, saturation and component-dependence make turbulence scaling richer than critical phenomena, and circulation tails may provide a more universal alternative observable.

examples.name: Burgers-equation/shock analogy
examples.note: Burgers turbulence is cited as the inspiration for the smaller-than-Kolmogorov cutoff idea and later as the model intuition for shock-like saturation, so it visibly feeds an idea rather than merely decorating it.
examples.engagement_reason: Burgers shocks are invoked as an analogy/inspiration for saturation and ramp-cliff behavior, but no Burgers calculation or construction is carried out.

passes.reason: Burgers shocks are pressureless/scalar limiting jump profiles used as an analogy for saturated, shock-like turbulence fluctuations, not to refute a claim.

examples.name: Gaussian random-field circulation control
examples.note: A Gaussian field with the same correlation function is used as a control showing that independent or weakly correlated vorticity sums would give Gaussian circulation statistics, contrasting with the observed exponential tails.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker runs the circulation statistics on Gaussian noise with the same correlation function and compares its PDF/collapse to the turbulent case.

passes.reason: The Gaussian random-field control has only covariance-level structure and no coherent higher cumulants, serving as a null comparison rather than a disproof.
The Gaussian random field is a maximally regular covariance-determined null control.
The Gaussian control is covariance-determined with vanishing higher cumulants, used as a null comparison for circulation PDFs rather than an adversarial example.
The Gaussian random field is the maximally regular second-order-determined control whose circulation remains Gaussian, used for comparison rather than contradiction.
The fixed-covariance Gaussian random field is a maximum-entropy null control, used as a foil for the turbulent circulation PDF.
The Gaussian field is a maximally regular Wick/CLT null model used to show that matching correlations would give Gaussian rather than observed exponential circulation tails
The Gaussian field with the same covariance is a maximally entropic, two-point-determined null model whose Gaussian circulation PDF rules out a Gaussian explanation of the observed tails.
The covariance-matched Gaussian field is a maximally regular/max-entropy control showing that covariance alone would give Gaussian rather than exponential circulation tails.
The Gaussian field is a highly regular null/control model for circulation statistics, not a refuting example itself.
The Gaussian random field is a highly regular covariance-determined null control used for comparison rather than as a counterexample.
Used as a negative Gaussian control showing a covariance-matched random field gives Gaussian rather than turbulent exponential circulation tails.
The same-covariance Gaussian random field is used as a max-entropy null control for circulation statistics, not to refute a claim.

examples.name: Inner boundary-layer scale nu over U
examples.note: In discussion an audience member observes that the proposed minimum scale can be written as the familiar shear-flow inner length nu/U, an analogy that clarifies the scale but does not generate a new step in the argument.
examples.engagement_reason: The proposed smallest scale is algebraically identified with nu/U and interpreted as the inner boundary-layer/shock-like cutoff scale controlling fine fluctuations.

examples.name: Passive-scalar temperature structure functions in convection
examples.note: An audience question brings in temperature/passive-scalar data whose exponents saturate earlier and whose ramp-cliff structures make the saturation mechanism concrete without being needed for the main turbulence claims.
examples.engagement_reason: In the discussion the passive-scalar temperature structure-function data are examined via their scaling exponents, saturation level, and ramp-cliff mechanism.

passes.reason: The passive-scalar temperature structure functions are ordinary turbulent scalar examples used to exhibit early saturation and departure from Kolmogorov-type scaling.
